q: What is defined as a celestial body or man-made object that orbits around a primary planet?
Which of Kepler's laws states that the orbit of a satellite is an ellipse with the primary body located at one of its foci?
Which of Kepler's laws states that a line segment joining a satellite and its primary sweeps out equal areas during equal intervals of time?
Which of Kepler's laws states that the square of the orbital period of a satellite is directly proportional to the cube of its semi-major axis?
What was the world's first artificial satellite, launched by the Soviet Union in October 1957?
What was the first active communications satellite launched, capable of receiving, amplifying, and retransmitting signals?
What was the first commercial geostationary communications satellite, also known as INTELSAT I, launched in 1965?
What is the approximate altitude of a geostationary (GEO) satellite above the Earth's equator?
How many operational satellites comprise the baseline GPS (NAVSTAR) constellation?
Into what orbital category are NAVSTAR GPS satellites classified?
What is the approximate orbital period of a NAVSTAR GPS satellite?
What is the primary carrier frequency used for the civilian GPS L1 signal?
What is the carrier frequency used for the military GPS L2 signal?
What type of satellite simply reflects radio signals back to Earth without providing any onboard amplification or processing?
What is another name for a transparent satellite transponder that amplifies and frequency-translates incoming signals without demodulating them?
What component is used in a satellite transponder to separate the wideband uplink spectrum into individual frequency channels?
What is the angle measured above the local horizontal plane to a satellite at the Earth station antenna?
What is the horizontal angle measured clockwise from true North to the satellite projection on the Earth's surface?
What are the two primary angles required to point an Earth station antenna directly toward a geostationary satellite?
What is the geographical region on the Earth's surface where a satellite's antenna beams can be received with sufficient strength?
What is the point on the Earth's surface representing the direction of maximum gain of a satellite's transmitting antenna?
When does a geostationary satellite experience orbital eclipses where the Earth blocks the sun's rays?
b: Twice a year during the vernal and autumnal equinoxes

q: What is the maximum duration of a satellite eclipse for a geostationary satellite?
Which battery type was widely used in early geostationary satellites to power subsystems during eclipses?
What is the primary power generation source for modern operational communications satellites during non-eclipse periods?
b: Silicon or Gallium Arsenide solar panels

q: What satellite stabilization method achieves orientation stability by physically rotating the outer body of the spacecraft?
What satellite stabilization method keeps the spacecraft's body fixed in space relative to Earth using internal momentum wheels?
What multiple access scheme allocates dedicated time slots on a single carrier frequency to different Earth stations?
What multiple access scheme allocates unique, dedicated frequency bands to different Earth stations within a transponder's bandwidth?
What type of channelization and allocation system is used in the SPADE satellite voice communications link?
What digital modulation technique is used for data transmission in the SPADE system?
What mathematical positioning technique is used by GPS receivers to calculate their exact physical coordinates using timing signals?
What high-accuracy GPS positioning service is reserved exclusively for military and authorized government personnel?
What GPS positioning service is available to all civilian users worldwide on a continuous basis, with no direct charge?
What is the typical downlink frequency range for a standard C-band satellite transponder?
What is the typical uplink frequency range for a standard C-band satellite transponder?
What is the typical downlink frequency range for a standard Ku-band satellite transponder?
What is the typical uplink frequency range for a standard Ku-band satellite transponder?
What highly elliptical, high-inclination orbit is widely used by northern countries to provide satellite coverage to polar regions?
What is the specific inclination angle used for a Molniya orbit to prevent apsidal drift?
What is the mathematical eccentricity of a perfectly circular satellite orbit?
What is the approximate inclination angle of a polar-orbiting satellite?
What term describes a satellite orbit that travels in the opposite direction of the Earth's rotation?
What term describes a satellite orbit that travels in the same direction as the Earth's rotation?
What is the point in a satellite's elliptical orbit that is closest to the Earth's surface?
What is the point in a satellite's elliptical orbit that is furthest from the Earth's surface?
What type of orbit remains fixed relative to the sun, allowing a satellite to pass over a given point on Earth at the same local solar time each day?
How many classical Keplerian orbital elements are required to completely specify a satellite's orbit in three-dimensional space?
What is the name of the doughnut-shaped regions of highly energetic trapped charged particles surrounding the Earth that satellites must avoid or be shielded against?
What spacecraft subsystem is responsible for monitoring satellite health parameters, tracking position, and receiving commands from the ground control station?
What is the primary purpose of guard times in a TDMA satellite transmission frame?
a: To provide a window for solar panel alignment
b: To prevent overlapping of burst transmissions from different Earth stations
c: To allow the local oscillators to stabilize between channels
d: To transmit parity check bits for forward error correction

q: What type of satellite transponder demodulates the received uplink signal down to the digital baseband level, processes or regenerates the data, and then modulates a new downlink carrier?
Which multiple access technique assigns different pseudo-random noise codes to different users, allowing them to share the same frequency band and time slot?
The term VSAT stands for which satellite communications network classification?
In a star-topology VSAT network, which station acts as the high-power central coordinator that relays all traffic between the small VSAT terminals?
What type of VSAT network topology allows direct terminal-to-terminal communications without passing through a central hub station?
What type of satellite orbital altitude category is characterized by orbits below 2,000 km, exhibiting short periods of 90 to 120 minutes?
Which of the following describes a sun-synchronous orbit altitude and inclination?
a: Altitude of 35,786 km, inclination of 0 degrees
b: Altitude of 600 to 800 km, inclination of approximately 98 degrees
c: Altitude of 20,200 km, inclination of 55 degrees
d: Highly eccentric altitude, inclination of 63.4 degrees

q: What is the primary function of a satellite's attitude control system?
a: To control the thermal temperature of the payload
b: To maintain the satellite's angular orientation and pointing direction toward Earth
c: To regulate the electrical voltage of the solar array
d: To switch channels in the input multiplexer

q: What represents the line of apsides of an elliptical satellite orbit?
a: The line connecting the ascending and descending nodes
b: The line connecting the apogee and perigee through the Earth's center
c: The line perpendicular to the equatorial plane
d: The trajectory path of the satellite's sub-point

q: The line of nodes in an orbital system is defined as the intersection of _____.
b: The satellite's orbital plane and the Earth's equatorial plane
d: The Greenwich meridian and the sub-satellite longitude

q: What Keplerian orbital element represents the angular distance of the ascending node measured eastward from the vernal equinox?
b: Right Ascension of the Ascending Node (RAAN)

q: What orbital parameter represents the angle measured from the ascending node to the perigee along the orbital plane?
a: Right Ascension of the Ascending Node

q: In satellite link design, what is the term for the ratio of the receiving antenna gain to the equivalent system noise temperature?
What represents the boresight of a satellite antenna?
a: The outer boundary of the -10 dB footprint
b: The physical axis of maximum radiation gain of the antenna
c: The mounting bracket of the feedhorn
d: The connection point to the travel-wave tube

q: Which multiple access scheme allows Earth stations to adjust their transmission rates and bandwidth dynamically based on real-time traffic demand?
What represents the major advantage of L-band frequencies over Ku-band frequencies for mobile satellite communications?
b: Negligible rain attenuation and atmospheric absorption

q: The orbit of a satellite that has an orbital period exactly equal to one sidereal day is called a _____.
What is the key difference between a geosynchronous orbit and a geostationary orbit?
a: A geosynchronous orbit has a period of 12 hours
b: A geostationary orbit is a circular geosynchronous orbit with an inclination angle of exactly 0 degrees
c: A geostationary orbit has a highly elliptical trajectory
d: Geosynchronous orbits do not suffer from eclipses

q: What are the thrusters on a satellite used for orbital correction and attitude maintenance collectively called?
b: Reaction Control System (RCS) or station-keeping thrusters

q: What is the term for the drift of a geostationary satellite from its assigned orbital slot, requiring periodic thruster firings?
What station-keeping correction counteracts the gravitational pull of the sun and the moon to maintain a geostationary satellite's 0-degree inclination?
What station-keeping correction counteracts the triaxiality of the Earth (the Earth's non-spherical shape) to prevent a geostationary satellite from drifting in longitude?
In satellite communications, what does 'dual polarization' (frequency reuse) refer to?
a: Transmitting on both C-band and Ku-band simultaneously
b: Using orthogonal polarizations (e.g., horizontal/vertical or LHCP/RHCP) to transmit two separate signals on the same frequency
c: Using space diversity at the Earth station
d: Demodulating the signal using both AM and FM detectors

q: What component in a satellite Earth station represents the first stage of RF amplification, having a very low noise contribution?
b: Low Noise Amplifier (LNA) or Low Noise Block (LNB)

q: Which microwave amplifier tube is widely used in high-power satellite transponders and Earth stations due to its high gain and wideband capabilities?
What represents the telemetry link of a satellite?
a: The uplink channel for sending commands to the spacecraft
b: The downlink channel for transmitting sensor data and status parameters of the spacecraft to the ground station
c: The voice channel in the SPADE system

q: What is the primary legislative or regulatory body responsible for coordinating international satellite orbital slot assignments and frequency allocations?
The orbital slot position of a geostationary satellite is specified in terms of _____.
A satellite is placed in an orbit with an eccentricity of e = 0.2 and a semi-major axis of a = 10,000 km. Calculate the radius of perigee (rp). Use rp = a * (1 - e).
A satellite is placed in an orbit with an eccentricity of e = 0.2 and a semi-major axis of a = 10,000 km. Calculate the radius of apogee (ra). Use ra = a * (1 + e).
A satellite elliptical orbit is measured to have an apogee radius of 15,000 km and a perigee radius of 9,000 km. Calculate the semi-major axis (a) of the orbit.
Using the orbital parameters from the previous question (ra = 15,000 km, rp = 9,000 km), calculate the mathematical eccentricity (e) of the orbit. Use e = (ra - rp) / (ra + rp).

A LEO satellite has an orbit with a semi-major axis of a = 7,000 km. Calculate its orbital velocity (v) in km/s assuming a circular orbit. Use Kepler's geocentric constant mu = 3.986 x 10^5 km^3/s^2. Use v = sqrt(mu / a).
A MEO satellite orbits at a radius of 26,500 km. Calculate its orbital velocity (v) in km/s.
Calculate the orbital velocity (v) of a geostationary satellite orbiting at a radius of 42,164 km.
A LEO satellite circular orbit has a semi-major axis of a = 8,000 km. Calculate its orbital period (T) in seconds. Use mu = 3.986 x 10^5 km^3/s^2. Use T = 2 * pi * sqrt(a^3 / mu).
Convert the orbital period of the LEO satellite from the previous question (T = 7,112 seconds) into minutes.
A circular orbit has a semi-major axis of a = 26,600 km. Calculate the orbital period (T) in hours.
A satellite is designed to have an orbital period of exactly 24 hours (86,400 seconds). Calculate the required semi-major axis (a) of the orbit in kilometers. Use mu = 3.986 x 10^5 km^3/s^2.
A satellite is designed to have an orbital period of exactly 12 hours (43,200 seconds) in a circular orbit. Calculate the required semi-major axis (a) of the orbit in kilometers.

A geostationary satellite transponder operates with an uplink frequency of 6 GHz. Calculate the free-space path loss (FSPL) in dB over a slant range distance of 38,000 km. Use FSPL = 32.44 + 20 * log(d_km) + 20 * log(f_MHz).
A geostationary satellite transponder operates with a downlink frequency of 4 GHz. Calculate the free-space path loss (FSPL) in dB over a slant range distance of 38,000 km.
A Ku-band satellite downlink operates at 12 GHz. Calculate the free-space path loss (FSPL) in dB over a slant range of 40,000 km.

A satellite earth station transmitter has a power output of 100 Watts (20 dBW). If it is connected to a parabolic dish antenna with a gain of 50 dBi, and the waveguide feeder loss is 2 dB, calculate the uplink EIRP in dBW.

Convert the uplink EIRP of 68 dBW from the previous question into Watts. (Context from previous question: A satellite earth station transmitter has a power output of 100 Watts (20 dBW). If it is connected to a parabolic dish antenna with a gain of 50 dBi, and the waveguide feeder loss is 2 dB, calculate the uplink EIRP in dBW.)

A satellite transponder has a downlink EIRP of 45 dBW. If the slant range path distance to the Earth station is 38,000 km (FSPL = 196.07 dB at 4 GHz) and atmospheric absorption loss is 0.5 dB, calculate the Received Signal Level (RSL) at the Earth station receiving antenna with a gain of 48 dBi.

Convert the Received Signal Level of -103.57 dBW from the previous question into decibel-milliwatts (dBm). (Context from previous question: A satellite transponder has a downlink EIRP of 45 dBW. If the slant range path distance to the Earth station is 38,000 km (FSPL = 196.07 dB at 4 GHz) and atmospheric absorption loss is 0.5 dB, calculate the Received Signal Level (RSL) at the Earth station receiving antenna with a gain of 48 dBi.)

An Earth station antenna with a physical diameter of 3.0 meters operates at 4 GHz (lambda = 0.075 m). Calculate the antenna power gain in dBi, assuming an aperture efficiency of 55%. Use G = eta * (pi * D / lambda)^2.
An Earth station antenna operates at 12 GHz (lambda = 0.025 m) and has a physical diameter of 1.2 meters. Calculate the antenna power gain in dBi, assuming an aperture efficiency of 55%.
Calculate the half-power beamwidth (-3 dB angle) of a 3.0-meter parabolic antenna operating at 4 GHz (lambda = 0.075 m). Use the practical approximation Beamwidth = 70 * lambda / D.
Calculate the half-power beamwidth (-3 dB angle) of a 1.2-meter parabolic antenna operating at 12 GHz (lambda = 0.025 m).

An Earth station receiving antenna has a gain of 45 dBi. If the equivalent system noise temperature of the receiver is 120 Kelvin, calculate the Earth station Figure of Merit (G/T) in dB/K. Use G/T = G_dBi - 10 * log10(T_Kelvin).
An Earth station receiving antenna has a gain of 48 dBi. If the system noise temperature is 150 Kelvin, calculate the Earth station Figure of Merit (G/T) in dB/K.

A low-noise block downconverter (LNB) has a noise temperature of 50 K. If the antenna feedline noise temperature is 20 K and the receiving antenna noise temperature is 30 K, calculate the total equivalent system noise temperature (T_system) at the input of the LNB, assuming no other lossy elements.

A satellite transponder operates at a downlink frequency of 4 GHz with a bandwidth of 36 MHz. If the Received Signal Level (RSL) at the Earth station is -103 dBW, and the system noise temperature is 120 K, calculate the total thermal noise power (Pn) in dBW. Use Pn_Watts = k * T * B, where k = 1.38 x 10^-23 J/K. Use k in dBW/K/Hz = -228.6 dBW/K/Hz.
For the satellite link in the previous question (RSL = -103 dBW, noise power Pn = -132.2 dBW), calculate the Carrier-to-Noise Ratio (C/N) in dB at the receiver input.
Convert the Carrier-to-Noise Ratio (C/N) of 29.2 dB over a 36 MHz bandwidth into the Carrier-to-Noise spectral density ratio (C/N0) in dB-Hz. Use C/N0 = C/N + 10 * log10(B).

An Earth station is designed with a receiving system Figure of Merit G/T = 20 dB/K. If the satellite downlink EIRP is 48 dBW and the path distance is 38,000 km (FSPL = 196.07 dB at 4 GHz), calculate the Carrier-to-Noise spectral density ratio (C/N0) in dB-Hz. Use C/N0 = EIRP - FSPL + G/T + 228.6.

For the satellite system in the previous question (C/N0 = 100.53 dB-Hz), the digital link transmits a data rate of 10 Mbps (70 dB-Hz). Calculate the Energy per Bit to Noise spectral density ratio (Eb/N0) in dB. Use Eb/N0 = C/N0 - 10 * log10(R).
A satellite transponder downlink has a frequency of 12 GHz. Calculate the free-space path loss (FSPL) in dB over a slant range of 38,000 km.

An Earth station receiver is located at a position where the slant range to the geostationary satellite is 37,500 km. Find the round-trip propagation time delay in milliseconds for a signal to go from Earth to satellite and back. (Use c = 3 x 10^8 m/s).

A GPS receiver is located at a position where the distance to a specific GPS satellite is 20,200 km. Calculate the propagation time delay in milliseconds for the GPS timing signal to reach the receiver.
A GPS receiver measures a timing delay of exactly 70.0 milliseconds from a GPS satellite. Calculate the pseudo-range distance to the satellite in kilometers.
A satellite earth station operates with an uplink antenna gain of 52 dBi and an amplifier output power of 50 Watts (17 dBW). If the waveguide loss is 1 dB, calculate the uplink EIRP in dBW.

Convert the uplink EIRP of 68 dBW from the previous question into Watts. (Context from previous question: A satellite earth station operates with an uplink antenna gain of 52 dBi and an amplifier output power of 50 Watts (17 dBW). If the waveguide loss is 1 dB, calculate the uplink EIRP in dBW.)

An Earth station antenna with a diameter of 2.4 meters has a receiving gain of 37.8 dBi at 4 GHz. Assuming the aperture efficiency is 55%, calculate the gain at 6 GHz using the relationship G2_dBi = G1_dBi + 20 * log10(f2/f1).

A satellite has a transmitter power output of 10 Watts (10 dBW) and an antenna gain of 15 dBi. Calculate the power flux density (F) in dBW/m^2 at a distance of 40,000 km in free space. Use F_dBW = EIRP_dBW - 10 * log10(4 * pi * d^2) where d is in meters.
Calculate the power flux density (F) in Watts per square meter for the satellite in the previous question (F = -138.01 dBW/m^2).
An Earth station receiving system has an LNA with a noise figure of 1.0 dB. Convert this noise figure into its equivalent absolute Noise Factor (F).
Using the absolute Noise Factor from the previous question (F = 1.259), calculate the equivalent noise temperature (Te) of the LNA in Kelvin. Use Te = T0 * (F - 1) where T0 = 290 Kelvin.

An Earth station receiver LNA has an equivalent noise temperature of 75 Kelvin. If it is connected to a receiver with a noise temperature of 145 Kelvin and a gain of 20 dB (absolute ratio 100), calculate the overall cascaded system noise temperature (T_system) at the input of the LNA.
If the Earth station in the previous question (T_system = 76.45 K) utilizes a receiving antenna with a gain of 48 dBi, calculate the Earth station Figure of Merit (G/T) in dB/K.

A satellite transponder downlink has a frequency of 12 GHz and a bandwidth of 50 MHz. If the Received Signal Level (RSL) at the Earth station is -105 dBW, and the system noise temperature is 150 K, calculate the total thermal noise power (Pn) in dBW. Use Pn_dBW = -228.6 + 10 * log10(T) + 10 * log10(B).
For the satellite link in the previous question (RSL = -105 dBW, noise power Pn = -129.8 dBW), calculate the Carrier-to-Noise Ratio (C/N) in dB at the receiver input.
Convert the Carrier-to-Noise Ratio (C/N) of 24.8 dB over a 50 MHz bandwidth into the Carrier-to-Noise spectral density ratio (C/N0) in dB-Hz.

An Earth station is designed with a receiving system Figure of Merit G/T = 25 dB/K. If the satellite downlink EIRP is 45 dBW and the path distance is 40,000 km (FSPL = 206.02 dB at 12 GHz), calculate the Carrier-to-Noise spectral density ratio (C/N0) in dB-Hz.

An elliptical satellite orbit has an apogee altitude of 10,000 km and a perigee altitude of 1,000 km above the Earth's surface. Given the Earth's radius is 6,378 km, calculate the semi-major axis (a) of the orbit.
